items.question_text: Under 9-6-1, what is the average ascent rate of an unmanned free balloon and its payload?
items.explanation: Per the note in 9-6-1, the balloon and payload ascend at an average rate of 400 feet per minute.

choices.text: 400 feet per minute.
1,000 feet per minute.
200 feet per minute.
800 feet per minute.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-1, what is the minimum descent rate of the payload after it is released from the balloon?
items.explanation: Per the note, the payload descends by parachute at a minimum rate of 1,000 feet per minute.

choices.text: At least 1,000 feet per minute by parachute.
400 feet per minute, the same as the ascent rate.
500 feet per minute by parachute.
2,000 feet per minute by free fall.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-1, how far in advance of descent is the operator required to advise ATC?
items.explanation: Per the note, the operator must advise ATC 1 hour in advance of descent in accordance with 14 CFR section 101.39.

choices.text: 30 minutes before expected descent.
1 hour in advance of descent.
Immediately before payload release.
2 hours in advance of descent.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-1, why do balloon transponders operate intermittently at cruise altitude?
items.explanation: Per the note, at cruise altitude the balloon's communications equipment and transponder are operated intermittently to conserve battery energy.

choices.text: To avoid interference with aircraft transponders at cruise altitudes.
To conserve battery energy.
Because 14 CFR 101.35 prohibits continuous transponder operation above 60,000 feet.
Because the transponder signal is unreliable above the tropopause.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-1c, when may a controller provide separation between aircraft and an unmanned free balloon?
items.explanation: Per 9-6-1c, separation may be provided with pilot concurrence AND when the controller is satisfied that balloon information is sufficiently reliable. Both conditions must be met.

choices.text: With pilot concurrence, when satisfied that balloon information is sufficiently reliable.
Whenever the balloon appears on radar, regardless of pilot input.
Only after the balloon operator guarantees position accuracy within 1 mile.
Automatically for all IFR aircraft within 5 miles of the balloon's estimated track.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-1c, when must a controller NOT use vertical separation from an unmanned free balloon?
items.explanation: Per 9-6-1c, do not attempt to separate aircraft from the balloon by using vertical separation unless you have accurate balloon altitude information.

choices.text: When the balloon's altitude is only estimated from its average climb rate.
Unless you have accurate balloon altitude information.
When the balloon is above FL 600.
Vertical separation may always be used; it is the preferred method.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-1d, what three elements must a traffic advisory for an unmanned free balloon include during initial contact?
items.explanation: Per 9-6-1d, advisories must specify the balloon's known or estimated position, direction of movement, and altitude as 'unknown' or 'reported.'

choices.text: Position (known/estimated), direction of movement, and altitude (unknown or reported).
Callsign, climb rate, and planned recovery zone.
Position, payload weight, and operator phone number.
Direction, groundspeed, and time since launch.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-1, how often are balloon operators required to record the balloon's position?
items.explanation: Per the note referencing 14 CFR 101.39, operators must monitor the course and record position at least every two hours. Position reports are not forwarded to ATC unless requested.

choices.text: At least every two hours.
Every 30 minutes.
Once per hour.
Only when crossing sector boundaries.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-1e, when transferring flight following of a balloon between facilities, which of the following must be forwarded when available?
items.explanation: Per 9-6-1e, the 7 items include identification/type, last known position/altitude, direction/speed, ETA over boundary, other pertinent info, radar point-out if in contact, and tracking location name/phone number.

choices.text: Identification/type, last position/altitude, direction/speed, ETA over boundary, other pertinent info, radar point-out, and tracking location name/phone.
Only the balloon's registration number and last reported altitude.
The balloon's callsign, operator contact, and payload weight only.
Only the ETA over the facility boundary and the chase plane frequency.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-1, fill in the blank: The balloon and payload ascend at an average rate of _____ feet per minute, and the payload descends by parachute at a minimum rate of _____ feet per minute.
items.explanation: Per the note: ascent is 400 ft/min average; descent is 1,000 ft/min minimum. These are distinct thresholds.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-1b, if radar flight following of an unmanned free balloon is NOT possible, which alternative tracking method is NOT listed?
items.explanation: 9-6-1b lists: chase plane communication, telephone contact with the operator, pilot reports, and ground observation reports. ADS-B tracking is not listed.

choices.text: ADS-B position reports from the balloon's transponder.
Communication with the chase plane.
Telephone contact with the operator.
Ground observation reports.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-2, what are the two conditions under which a balloon becomes derelict?
items.explanation: Per 9-6-2, a balloon becomes derelict when (1) a moored balloon slips its mooring and becomes a hazard to air navigation, or (2) an unmanned free balloon flight cannot be terminated as planned.

choices.text: When a moored balloon slips its mooring and becomes a hazard, or when an unmanned free balloon flight cannot be terminated as planned.
Whenever a balloon is out of radar contact for more than 10 minutes.
Only when the balloon operator declares an emergency and requests derelict status.
When any balloon exceeds its planned flight duration by more than 2 hours.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-2a, what is the required action when a moored balloon has slipped its moorings?
items.explanation: Per 9-6-2a, issue traffic advisories. Unlike unmanned free balloons, there is no requirement to flight follow or provide separation.

choices.text: Issue traffic advisories.
Flight follow the balloon and provide separation to all aircraft.
Broadcast a derelict balloon advisory at 3-minute intervals.
Immediately vector all traffic at least 5 miles away.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-2, how does the controller response differ between a derelict moored balloon and a derelict unmanned free balloon?
items.explanation: Per 9-6-2a, moored balloons: issue traffic advisories only. Per 9-6-2b, unmanned free balloons: flight follow AND provide separation to the extent possible. The free balloon response is more involved.

choices.text: Moored: issue traffic advisories. Unmanned free: flight follow and provide separation to the extent possible.
Both types require identical handling: flight follow, separation, and 10-minute advisory broadcasts.
Moored: provide separation and broadcast advisories. Unmanned free: issue traffic advisories only.
Neither type requires controller action unless a pilot reports the balloon in sight.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-2, whom does the controller forward balloon position information to for further dissemination?
items.explanation: Per 9-6-2c, forward balloon position information received from pilot reports or derived from radar returns to your supervisor for further dissemination.

choices.text: To your supervisor for further dissemination.
Directly to all aircraft on frequency via broadcast.
To the balloon operator for their records.
To the Flight Standards District Office.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-2d, if radar contact with a derelict balloon is lost, at what interval must advisories be broadcast, and for how long?
items.explanation: Per 9-6-2d, broadcast at 10-minute intervals until the derelict balloon is no longer a factor.

choices.text: At 10-minute intervals until the derelict balloon is no longer a factor.
At 3-minute intervals until the balloon lands.
Once, then stop broadcasting and wait for pilot reports.
At 5-minute intervals for 30 minutes, then only if requested by a pilot.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-2, how is flight following responsibility for a derelict balloon transferred?
items.explanation: Per 9-6-2e, transfer flight following responsibility as outlined in paragraph 9-6-1, Application, subparagraph e. This refers back to the 7-item transfer list.

choices.text: As outlined in paragraph 9-6-1, subparagraph e.
Using the standard handoff procedures in Chapter 4.
By notifying the balloon operator who then contacts the receiving facility.
No transfer is required; each facility handles derelict balloons independently.

items.question_text: Under 9-6-2, fill in the blank: If radar contact with the derelict balloon is lost, broadcast an advisory at _____ intervals continuing until the derelict balloon is no longer a factor.
items.explanation: The prescribed interval is 10 minutes — a different interval from the 3-minute fuel-dumping interval in 9-4-5.

choices.text: 10-minute
3-minute
5-minute
15-minute

items.situation: An unmanned free balloon is in your airspace. The operator provides only approximate position data and cannot give an accurate altitude. A trainee wants to separate IFR traffic from the balloon by assigning 2,000 feet above the balloon's estimated altitude based on its average climb rate.
items.question_text: Can you use vertical separation in this situation?

choices.text: No. 9-6-1c prohibits vertical separation unless you have accurate balloon altitude information. An estimated altitude from average climb rate is not accurate enough.
Yes. The average 400 ft/min climb rate allows controllers to estimate altitude accurately enough for vertical separation.
Yes. Any balloon altitude information, even estimated, meets the 9-6-1c standard for vertical separation.
No. Vertical separation from balloons is prohibited in all cases under 9-6-1c.

items.explanation: Per 9-6-1c, do not attempt to separate aircraft from the balloon by using vertical separation unless you have accurate balloon altitude information.

items.situation: You are providing traffic advisories for an unmanned free balloon. The trainee issues 'Traffic, unmanned free balloon, 12 o'clock, 10 miles, eastbound.' The balloon's position is estimated over the PANBE VOR and its last reported altitude was FL 310.
items.question_text: Is the trainee's advisory complete per 9-6-1d?

choices.text: No. 9-6-1d requires specifying the balloon's known or estimated position, direction of movement, AND altitude as unknown or reported. The trainee omitted the altitude.
Yes. Position and direction are sufficient for unmanned free balloons because altitude is always reported as unknown.
Yes. The trainee included all three required elements: position, direction, and altitude are optional for balloons.
No. 9-6-1d only requires position and distance; direction and altitude are not required elements for balloon advisories.

items.explanation: Per 9-6-1d, traffic advisories must specify position (known or estimated), direction of movement, AND altitude (as unknown or reported). All three are required.

items.situation: A derelict unmanned free balloon was being flight followed, but radar contact has been lost. A trainee says to just forward the last known position to the supervisor and stop broadcasting because the balloon can't be tracked anymore.
items.question_text: What additional action is required under 9-6-2?

choices.text: Broadcast an advisory to all aircraft in the affected airspace at 10-minute intervals until the derelict balloon is no longer a factor.
No additional action; forwarding position to the supervisor is sufficient when radar contact is lost.
Immediately transfer control to the next facility because a lost-radar balloon is no longer your responsibility.
Suspend all operations in the affected airspace until the balloon is relocated.

items.explanation: Per 9-6-2d, when radar contact is lost, broadcast advisories at 10-minute intervals until the derelict balloon is no longer a factor. This is in addition to forwarding position information to the supervisor (9-6-2c).

items.situation: A moored balloon at a local festival has slipped its moorings and is drifting into controlled airspace. A trainee begins flight following the balloon and preparing to provide separation to IFR traffic.
items.question_text: Is the trainee's response correct under 9-6-2?

choices.text: No. For derelict moored balloons, 9-6-2a requires only traffic advisories. Flight following and separation are for derelict unmanned free balloons under 9-6-2b.
Yes. All derelict balloons require flight following and separation regardless of type.
No. Moored balloons that slip moorings are handled under 9-6-1, not 9-6-2.
Yes. But the trainee should also broadcast advisories at 3-minute intervals.

items.explanation: Per 9-6-2a, a moored balloon which has slipped its moorings requires only traffic advisories. Flight following and separation apply to derelict unmanned free balloons per 9-6-2b.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.question_text: Identify the incorrect word: ADVISORY TO ALL AIRCRAFT. DERELICT BALLOON REPORTED ABOVE THE VICINITY OF GAGE.
items.original_phrase: ADVISORY TO ALL AIRCRAFT. DERELICT BALLOON REPORTED IN THE VICINITY OF GAGE.
items.display_text: ADVISORY TO ALL AIRCRAFT DERELICT BALLOON REPORTED ABOVE THE VICINITY OF GAGE
items.explanation: Per 9-6-2 phraseology, the advisory says 'REPORTED IN THE VICINITY OF (location),' not 'ABOVE THE VICINITY OF.'
